Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 55 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
The UP actions stopped early after 5 actions (noop_short_circuit). The blue squares are still at:
- Left: rows 39-43, cols 19-23
- Right: rows 44-48, cols 39-43
They didn't move! The border went from 42 B's to 40 B's (was 42 B's and 22 W's, now 40 B's and 24 W's). Wait, the output says "Top row: 43 B's, 21 W's", which is the same as before. So the border didn't change.
Wait, the previous output said "Top row: 43 B's, 21 W's" before the UP actions. After the UP actions, the output says "Top row: 43 B's, 21 W's". So the border didn't change.
But the blue squares are still at the same positions. So the UP actions didn't move the blue squares.
This means the blue squares are at their maximum upward position. They can't move up anymore.
So the blue squares are at:
- Left: rows 39-43 (can't move up further)
- Right: rows 44-48 (can't move up further)
Wait, but the left blue square moved from rows 54-58 to rows 39-43 after 5 UP actions. And then after 5 more UP actions, it didn't move. So the left blue square is at its maximum upward position (rows 39-43).
Similarly, the right blue square moved from rows 54-58 to rows 44-48 after 5 UP actions. And then after 5 more UP actions, it didn't move. So the right blue square is at its maximum upward position (rows 44-48).
So the blue squares have reached their maximum upward positions. The left blue square is at rows 39-43, and the right blue square is at rows 44-48.
Now I need to figure out the goal. The blue squares are at different rows, creating an asymmetric face. Maybe the goal is to make the blue squares align at the same row.
Let me try pressing DOWN to move the blue squares down. If I press DOWN, the left blue square will move from rows 39-43 to rows 44-48 (5 rows down), and the right blue square will move from rows 44-48 to rows 49-53 (5 rows down).
Wait, but the blue squares move at different speeds. The left blue square moves 3 rows per UP action, and the right blue square moves 2 rows per UP action. So the left blue square moves faster than the right blue square.
If I press DOWN, the left blue square will move slower than the right blue square (or vice versa). Let me try pressing DOWN to see what happens.
Actually, I think the issue is that I don't understand the movement pattern. Let me re-examine the transitions.
Before the first 5 UP actions, both blue squares were at rows 54-58. After 5 UP actions, the left blue square is at rows 39-43 and the right blue square is at rows 44-48.
So the left blue square moved 15 rows up (54 - 39 = 15), and the right blue square moved 10 rows up (54 - 44 = 10).
This means the left blue square moved 3 rows per UP action (15 / 5 = 3), and the right blue square moved 2 rows per UP action (10 / 5 = 2).
But wait, the blue squares are 5x5. So the left blue square moved from rows 54-58 to rows 39-43, which is a shift of 15 rows (54 - 39 = 15). The right blue square moved from rows 54-58 to rows 44-48, which is a shift of 10 rows (54 - 44 = 10).
So the left blue square moved 15 rows up, and the right blue square moved 10 rows up. This is a difference of 5 rows.
I think the issue is that the blue squares have different "speeds" based on their column position. The left blue square is at cols 19-23, and the right blue square is at cols 39-43.
Actually, let me think about this differently. Maybe the blue squares move to specific "slots" within the black shape. The black shape has a specific layout with "slots" at different rows.
Looking at the ascii, the face has:
- A black outline at rows 9-58.
- Yellow region on the left.
- Orange region on the right.
- The blue squares are inside the black shape.
The black shape has "slots" at rows 14-18 (eye sockets), rows 19-38 (middle), and rows 39-58 (mouth).
The blue squares are currently at rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right), which is in the "mouth" region.
But the blue squares can't move up further. So maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the same row.
Let me try pressing DOWN to move the blue squares down. If I press DOWN, the left blue square will move from rows 39-43 to rows 44-48 (5 rows down), and the right blue square will move from rows 44-48 to rows 49-53 (5 rows down).
Wait, but the blue squares move at different speeds. The left blue square moves 3 rows per UP action, and the right blue square moves 2 rows per UP action. So the left blue square moves faster than the right blue square when going up.
When going down, the left blue square might move slower than the right blue square (or vice versa). Let me try pressing DOWN to see what happens.
Actually, I think the goal might be to make the blue squares align at the same row. Currently, the left blue square is at rows 39-43 and the right blue square is at rows 44-48. They're 5 rows apart.
If I press DOWN, the left blue square will move down, and the right blue square will move down. If the left blue square moves slower than the right blue square when going down, they might align at the same row.
Let me try pressing DOWN 5 times to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Top row: {ascii_lines[0].count('B')} B's, {ascii_lines[0].count('W')} W's")

nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue square id={node['id']} bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")

# The blue squares are at different rows. Let me try pressing DOWN to align them.
# If the left blue square moves slower than the right blue square when going down, they might align.
action(['DOWN'] * 5)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Blue square id=5 bbox=(39,19)-(43,23)
Blue square id=6 bbox=(44,39)-(48,43)
